prompt:
Observability ticket. Our batch jobs push custom metrics to Datadog through a crusty shell script that curls the ancient v1 series endpoint one metric at a time — it ignores payload limits, sends everything uncompressed, silently drops the intake's error responses, and hardcodes the US1 site so our EU org gets nothing. Build a proper Python package `ddmetrics` (stdlib only) that submits metrics through the CURRENT Datadog API v2 series operation. test_datadog_metrics.py is the acceptance contract.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the current official Datadog documentation — at minimum the API reference page for the v2 submit-metrics operation (exact path, auth header, series/points/resources schema and the integer metric-type enum, point timestamp freshness rules, payload size limits, compression options, success and error envelopes) and the official page listing Datadog sites and how a site maps to its API host. Datadog's rate-limits reference and the official datadog-api-client sources on GitHub also count as first-party. Only official Datadog pages count as sources; blogs and Stack Overflow do not. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests use:
- ddmetrics.sites: KNOWN_SITES plus resolve_api_base(site) mapping a documented site parameter to its API base URL; unknown or malformed sites raise ValueError.
- ddmetrics.series: the documented integer type constants (UNSPECIFIED/COUNT/RATE/GAUGE) and make_series(metric, mtype, points, tags=None, resources=None, unit=None, interval=None, source_type_name=None) -> dict that normalizes points given as (ts, value) pairs or dicts into the v2 object form, validates the type enum, requires interval for the types the docs say need one, validates resources entries, and omits unset optionals entirely.
- ddmetrics.batch: MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES and MAX_DECOMPRESSED_BYTES pinned to the documented limits, and split_series(series, max_bytes=MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES) that packs series in order into as few payloads as fit by compact-serialized JSON size, raising ValueError when a single series alone cannot fit.
- ddmetrics.client.MetricsClient(api_key, site="datadoghq.com", base_url=None, compression=None, now=<injected>, sleep=<injected>, max_retries=2): submit(series) POSTs each payload to the documented v2 path with the documented auth header (header only — never a query param, and no application key), Content-Type and Accept application/json. compression may be None, "deflate" (zlib) or "gzip"; any other value — including the third documented encoding this client does not implement — raises ValueError naming it. Compressed submission packs batches by the documented decompressed-size limit while keeping the wire bytes within the payload cap. Point timestamps outside the documented freshness window raise ValueError naming the metric BEFORE any request is sent. 429 responses sleep exactly the Retry-After seconds via the injected sleep and retry the same payload up to max_retries times before recording the failure. Every payload gets its own outcome: submit returns {submitted, accepted, ok, batches:[{status, series_count, errors, attempts}]} where a payload failure (or a 202 that still carries errors) never blocks later payloads, and accepted counts only series from clean 202 batches. The API key must never appear in the report, exceptions, or logs. Never time.sleep.

Acceptance: python3 test_datadog_metrics.py — it spins up a loopback fake Datadog intake; no real Datadog, no real credentials. test_datadog_metrics.py and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

official_sources.title: Submit metrics — Datadog API Reference
official_sources.used_for: POST /api/v2/series contract: DD-API-KEY auth, Content-Type application/json, optional Content-Encoding, series schema (metric, integer type enum 0=unspecified/1=count/2=rate/3=gauge, points as {timestamp,value} objects, resources {name,type}, tags, unit, interval for rate/count), point freshness (not >10min future, not >1h past), payload limits (512000 bytes; compressed payloads must decompress to <5242880 bytes), 202 {"errors":[]} success and 400/403/408/413/429 error envelope

official_sources.title: Getting Started with Datadog Sites
official_sources.used_for: site parameter values (datadoghq.com, us3.datadoghq.com, us5.datadoghq.com, datadoghq.eu, ap1.datadoghq.com, ap2.datadoghq.com, ddog-gov.com) and that each independent site is reached at https://api.<site>

official_sources.title: Rate Limits — Datadog API Reference
official_sources.used_for: X-RateLimit-Limit/Period/Remaining/Reset/Name header family, 429 semantics and retry guidance; note that metric point submission itself is not rate limited

official_sources.title: datadog-api-client-python MetricContentEncoding model (official Datadog client source)
official_sources.used_for: the exact allowed Content-Encoding values for v2 metric submission: deflate, zstd1, gzip

research.language_rationale: The metrics pipeline this submitter replaces is Python. Datadog's official datadog-api-client-python is a generated wrapper over this same v2 REST endpoint, and the verify environment is stdlib-only, so urllib.request against the documented POST /api/v2/series contract is the credible dependency-free integration surface; the REST reference is Datadog's primary documented interface.
